coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modification on hydrangea-like In2O3 for ppb-level formaldehyde detection: Experimental performance and theoretical insight

Wang, Shihan

coredata.dc:description: With the growing demands on environmental protection and human health, numerous efforts have been devoted to the high-efficiency detection of formaldehyde gas. Herein, a simple and green manner was adopted to generate a 3D/0D heterogeneous structure. It was found that the morphology evolution of the In2O3-based sample could be fine-tuned through changing the addition concentration. Impressively, the unique hydrangea-like structure was assembled by connecting In2O3 nanosheets. Furthermore, the carbon quantum dots were incorporated to upgrade the performance. The hydrangea-like IO-2@CQDs, hence, exhibits remarkable sensitivity, excellent response repeatability, superior selectivity as well as reliable long-term stability at 160 °C. An outstanding limit of detection can be as low as 86.23 ppb. In addition, the corresponding CQDs sensitization mechanism was comprehensively elaborated with experimental performance, DFT calculations (16 models, 41 images), and surface depletion theory. The active surface sites, n-p hetero-interface, and unique hydrangea-like morphology account for the boosted HCHO-sensitive performance. This work not only highlights the promising combination of metal oxides and CQDs but also provides valuable inspiration and theoretical perspectives for 3D/0D heterojunction construction.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modification on hydrangea-like In2O3 for ppb-level formaldehyde detection: Experimental performance and theoretical insight

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the growing demands on environmental protection and human health, numerous efforts have been devoted to the high-efficiency detection of formaldehyde gas. Herein, a simple and green manner was adopted to generate a 3D/0D heterogeneous structure. It was found that the morphology evolution of the In2O3-based sample could be fine-tuned through changing the addition concentration. Impressively, the unique hydrangea-like structure was assembled by connecting In2O3 nanosheets. Furthermore, the carbon quantum dots were incorporated to upgrade the performance. The hydrangea-like IO-2@CQDs, hence, exhibits remarkable sensitivity, excellent response repeatability, superior selectivity as well as reliable long-term stability at 160 °C. An outstanding limit of detection can be as low as 86.23 ppb. In addition, the corresponding CQDs sensitization mechanism was comprehensively elaborated with experimental performance, DFT calculations (16 models, 41 images), and surface depletion theory. The active surface sites, n-p hetero-interface, and unique hydrangea-like morphology account for the boosted HCHO-sensitive performance. This work not only highlights the promising combination of metal oxides and CQDs but also provides valuable inspiration and theoretical perspectives for 3D/0D heterojunction construction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the accelerating development of large-scale industrial production and fossil fuel combustion, the emissions of harmful and toxic gaseous pollutants have increased, and human beings will have to face the risks of exposure to volatile gases. Its carcinogenicity, teratogenicity, and mutagenicity would cause potentially harmful effects on human health. As a representative raw material, formaldehyde has critical applications in wood fixative, indoor building materials, daily cosmetics, construction, and the textile industry. Due to its day-by-day uses, prolonged exposure to high concentrations of formaldehyde may pose serious health hazards to the human body, i.e., cardiovascular disease, hepatic damage, asthma, and even cancer [1,2]. Therefore, the Occupational Safety Health and Administration (OSHA) regulates the permissible exposure level of formaldehyde at 0.75 ppm [3]. Given the stricter environmental pollution monitoring and control standards worldwide, the growing demand for formaldehyde gas sensors is continuously increasing. Particularly, highly sensitive and selective HCHO sensor need to be developed in a steady and efficient manner.

During the past thirty decades, technological revolutions have hit the nanomaterials field. Gas sensors based on nano-scale metal oxide semiconductors (MOSs), commonly called electronic noses, have been extensively investigated in air quality monitoring. The reversible variation in conductivity induced by gas–solid interactions can be converted into electrical signals, which are further transformed into digital signals to evaluate sensing performance for the analyte gas. These MOSs-based gas sensors have presumed greater significance because of their merits of cost-effectiveness, easy manufacturing, tunable properties and long service life [3–5]. A mass of n-type (e.g., ZnO, Fe2O3, MoO3, SnO2, WO3) and p-type MOSs (e.g., NiO, CuO, Co3O4) have been successfully deemed as effective sensing materials for detection of toxic, explosive and flammable VOCs, especially for formaldehyde detection [4,5]. As a vital n-type MOSs with a narrowed band gap, In2O3 possesses good optical and electrical properties, low toxicity, and high stability [6,7]. Unfortunately, unsatisfactory performances, exceptionally low sensitivity, and poor selectivity have hindered its detection limits and further application. Bulk, granular, or monolayer nanosheets of In2O3 with low specific surface area cannot exhibit sufficient adsorption capability, and the high operating temperature may lead to agglomeration and continued growth of oxide particles, resulting in poor selectivity and long-term stability.

To date, the major concern in MOSs is to continue to ameliorate its sensing performance. Generally, there are four strategies as follows: (1) synthesizing nanomaterials with smaller grain size, high porosity, and surface area; (2) constructing hierarchical, hollow, flower-like and hydrangea-like microstructures [8]; (3) generating n-p heterojunction, n-n and p-p homojunction or Ohmic contact; (4) modifying MOSs by doping transition-metal ions (Cr, Co, Ni, Fe and Cu, etc), noble metal ions (Y, Pd, Pt, Au and Rh, etc) or quantum dots (QDs) [9,10]. Available data suggest that the gas-sensing properties of metal oxide semiconductors can be affected by the controlled morphology and heterogeneous structure. Hydrangea-like microstructure can provide a larger specific surface area, abundant adsorption sites and diffusion pathways, efficient electron transfer and enhanced utility factor, which is also benefit for doping components to boost the sensing performance [5,11]. On the other hand, QDs modification have attracted a great deal of attention. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively researched and widely constructed owing to its low-cost character, high electronic properties, ultra-small size and rapid electron transfer [12]. Various functional groups (e.g., COH, COOH, CO) make CQDs have high compatibility with other active nanomaterials. Moreover, the addition of CQDs not only introduces carbon–oxygen functional groups or generates some reactive functional groups (e.g., Ti-O in CQDs/TiO2) at the CQDs/MOSs interface, roughening the surface and increasing the surface area to promote gas adsorption, but also serves as a bridge that making it easier for electrons to be transferred between the MOSs surface and gas molecules. Therefore, the CQDs/MOSs interface is conductive to improving the electrons transport and enlarging the contact area, which is beneficial for the combination of QDs and MOSs to generate heterojunctions and for utilizing the heterogeneous synergistic effect of CQDs/MOSs contact interface to improve the sensing performance [13–15]. For instance, CQDs decorated TiO2 nanofibers (CQDs/TiO2) generates reactive Ti-O groups. The CQDs/SnO2 interface possess an improved detection limit (0.5 ppm), brilliant selectivity, increased response/recovery rate (45/60 s) and good long-term stability to formaldehyde under UV light at room temperature [15]. Similarly, the unique Lychee-like SnO2 hollow microspheres with CQDs modification were controllably prepared. In comparison with pure SnO2, excellent electron transfer at the CQDs/SnO2 interface leads to a higher response (28 vs 12), superior selectivity and faster response speed (9 s vs 36 s) for 100 ppm ethanol at 216 °C [16]. Based on the unique affinity, CQDs may be a promising candidate as the guest materials for sensing application. To solve the mentioned issues of pristine In2O3, constructing hydrangea In2O3-based sensors with carbon quantum dots modification may be considered to be an effective strategy for improved electron transfer and high sensing performance. It is also highly desirable to be able to connect hydrangea In2O3-based sensors to the Internet-of-Things (IoT) and artificial intelligence (AI) in the near future.----

Herein, inthefollowingwork, we made an attempt to synthesize hydrangea-like In2O3 through a facile hydrothermal chemical approach, which was followed by thermal decomposition and self-assembly strategy. Then, the carbon quantum dots modified In2O3 hydrangea were acquired. Notably, the synthesis conditions affect the material morphology evolution. Hydrangea-like morphology and CQDs modification both alter the gas-sensing characteristics. Compared with the pure In2O3 sensor, the 3D/0D heterojunction (IO-2@CQDs) shows impressive enhancement in low-temperature HCHO-sensing performance, such as higher response, improved selectivity, and lower detection limit. Furthermore, comprehensive characterization and density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to investigate the possible sensitization mechanism. It suggests that CQDs modification not only strengthens the adsorption ability but also enhances the electron transfer capability for oxygen and formaldehyde molecules. Our research reveals that the CQDs modification may be a promising strategy for boosting gas-sensing performance, which opens venues to developing advanced In2O3-based gas sensors.

The used reagents during the entire experiment, i.e., glucose (C6H12O6), ascorbic acid (C6H8O6), and ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Indium nitrate hydrate (InN3O9⋅xH2O) and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Aladdin Ltd (Shanghai, China). All the chemicals were commercial analytical grade (AR) and utilized without further purification. Deionized water (DIW) was used throughout the following procedure.

Briefly, 0.018 mol C6H12O6 was dissolved into 60 ml DIW under continuous stirring. After that, the obtained homogenous transparent solution was transferred and sealed into a 100 ml Teflon autoclave, then kept heating at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the black precipitates were centrifuged and washed with anhydrous ethanol and DIW several times, then dried at 60 °C overnight. The corresponding synthesis process is shown in Fig. S1 (Supplementary Material).

In a typical synthesis procedure, 0.2 g carbon sphere template were firstly added to 100 ml beaker with 40 ml anhydrous ethanol. Then the beaker was sealed and sonicated for 0.5 h. Afterwards, 0.15 mmol InN3O9⋅xH2O were added into the obtained suspension and stirred for 2 h. The mixed suspension was then transferred into Teflon autoclave, which was hydrothermally maintained at 180 °C for 8 h (Fig. 1 a). Eventually, the as-synthesized In2O3 precursors were calcined at 500 °C for 2 h under an air atmosphere to obtain an IO-2 sample. Another two samples were also synthesized by the same protocol except for the different molar of indium nitrate (0.1 and 0.25 mmol). For a matter of convenience, these two samples were sequentially noted as IO-1, and IO-3, respectively.

As previously reported, 2.0 g C6H8O6 and 20 ml C2H6O2 were dissolved in 30 ml deionized water to form the homogenous solution. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 ml) and kept at 160 °C for 70 min. After natural cooling to room temperature, the obtained CQDs suspension (0.167 g/ml) can be used in the following synthesis procedure [17].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs modification on hydrangea-like In2O3
ce:section.ce:para: The synthetic procedure of hybrid nanocomposites is illustrated in Fig. 1b. For In2O3/CQDs 3D/0D hybrids, the 100 mg of prepared IO-2 hydrangea was firstly dispersed in 30 ml anhydrous ethanol to form suspension A. 5 ml anhydrous ethanol and 1 ml CQDs suspension were mixed to form the uniformly dispersed suspension B. 60 μl of suspension B was slowly dropped into suspension A under stirring. The obtained suspension was treated with 15 min ultrasonication and then stirred at room temperature for 3 h. Finally, the mixture suspension is centrifuged, washed, and then dried overnight to obtain IO-2@CQDs composite.

Thermogravimetry-Derivative Thermogravimetry (TG-DTG) was investigated by TG 209 F1 (German) thermal analyzer in the ambient air. X-ray diffraction analysis (Rigaku D/Max-2400, Rigaku) with a high-intensity Cu-Kα1 radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) was applied to examine the crystal structure. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Nicolet iS5, Thermo) and Raman spectroscopy (Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution, Hitachi) were used for molecular structure analysis. The microscopic morphology and internal microstructure of obtained samples were investigated by scanning electron microscope (SEM, XL30FEG, Philips) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-F200, JEOL) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Moreover, the specific surface area and pore size information were obtained by the nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherm (Autosorb-iQ2 apparatus, Quantachrome) according to Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods. The oxygen temperature-programmed desorption (O2-TPD) with a mass spectrometer (DECRA, Hiden) and the thermal conductivity detector (TCD) were further used to analyzed the oxygen species on the surface. The Mott-Schottky (M−S) and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were obtained through the CHI660E electrochemical workstation with a standard three electrode set-up. The absorption spectra were tested from the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) optical spectrophotometer (U4150, Hitachi).

The detailed schematic diagram of gas sensing devices is shown in Fig. S2 [18]. Prior to the gas-sensing test, a certain amount of powdered samples was uniformly dispersed into anhydrous ethanol to form the homogeneous suspension. Subsequently, the suspension was pipetted and drop-coated onto a commercial Al2O3 ceramic tube. A pair of parallel gold electrodes and four platinum (Pt) wires are attached to the surface of the ceramic tube. Meanwhile, a Ni-Cr wire was inserted into the ceramic tube to control the operating temperature by adjusting the voltage. Based on previous reports, carbon or CQDs-modified composites enable reproducible gas-sensitive measurements in the range of 25–300 °C [12,17,19]. After keeping thermally aged for several days, the sensing performance of various sensing devices was evaluated on the WS-30A measurement equipment under laboratory conditions (25 ± 2 °C, 35 ± 2 % RH). The ambient relative humidity (35 %-80 % RH) is also conducted at room temperature (Supplementary Material). The corresponding injected gas concentration was obtained according to the defined formula of Eq. (1)

in which, Q, C, M, Vc , d, ρ, Tt , Tc represent the injected liquid volume (ml), desired gas concentration (ppm), molecular weight, volume of gas chamber (18 L), density of liquid (g/cm3), purity of liquid, the test ambient temperature (°C) and gas chamber temperature (°C), respectively.

Theoretical calculations based on DFT were performed through Materials Studio (MS). To construct the adsorption geometry structure, generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Perdue-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) function was employed to describe the exchange–correlation potential. A 20 Å vacuum space in the z-direction was used to avoid periodic interactions. Subsequently, the 4 × 4 × 1 Monkhorst-centered k-point grid was applied for slab structure optimization in the Brillouin zone. Then, the spin unrestricted was considered for all the adsorption systems. Moreover, the DFT-D correction proposed by Grimme was adopted to describe the van der Waals function. The convergence criteria of slab structure were chosen as the energy, force, and displacement less than 1 × 10-5 Ha, 2 × 10-3 Ha/Å and 5 × 10-3 Å, respectively. Finally, 11 geometry model has been established and then calculated.

The calculated adsorption energy (E ads) can be defined as follows:
(2)

in which E slab+gas is the total energy of slab after adsorption of gas molecules (O2 and HCHO); E slab is the total energy of the clean surface of slab; and E gas is the energy of the free gas molecules, separately.

The TG-DTG curve (Fig. S3) was investigated to demonstrate the decomposition characteristics of precursor sample during calcination under air atmosphere with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. It can be seen that the first mass loss (2.96 %) appeared in the 30 °C to 180 °C range, which can be assigned to the adsorbed water evaporation [1]. And then, a rapid mass loss occurred suddenly in the range of 180–350 °C. It is worthy to note that the largest velocity of mass loss happened at 350 °C. It may be attribute to the decomposition of carbon template [20,21]. The third gradual weight loss stage (350–460 °C) indicates the crystalline transformation of In2O3 [18,22]. Eventually, the weight of the sample no longer changes when the temperature is further increased to the high-temperature region (460–800 °C). Therefore, 500 °C is selected as the calcination temperature to prepared In2O3-based sample.

The XRD patterns of the as-acquired samples were captured to investigate the crystalline structural details. As displayed in Fig. 2 a–b, all of the In2O3 products display the well-resolved characteristic 21.42°, 30.48°, 35.38°, 37.50°, 41.72°, 45.62°, 50.98°, 55.82°, 60.66°, and 67.84° diffraction peaks with a cubic phase, exactly matching with the (211), (222), (400), (411), (332), (431), (400), (611), (622) and (633) crystal planes, according to standard PDF card (JCPDS # 71–2194). The characteristic peaks of the IO-2@CQDs sample also display the same crystallographic properties of the cubic phase (space group: Ia-3(206)). Subsequently, we can see that the characteristic peaks pertaining to carbon quantum dots in IO-2@CQDs spectra are not obvious, which can be attributed to the low doping amount. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 2a, three XRD peaks have the rule of increasing peak intensity, which is due to the increment of addition concentration. No diffraction peaks corresponding to any other impurities can be found in all the spectra, indicating that the obtained samples are of high purity.

The microscopic morphology and surface microstructure of the five synthetic products were revealed by SEM measurements first. As can be seen in Fig. S4, tiny and smooth carbon microspheres present a high degree of homogeneity, monodispersity, and perfect solid spherical structure with a diameter of 100–115 nm, which is beneficial for further growth of active nanomaterial. Due to the insufficient addition of Indium nitrate hydrate, the high magnification SEM image of IO-1 (Fig. 3 b) reveals a high-density growth of irregularly shaped micro-structure, which indicates that conjoined In2O3 nanoparticles are all tightly aggregated. The insufficient additions prevent them from continuing to grow into nanosheets during the Ostwald ripening process but only encapsulate on the surface of the sphere templates, finally forming irregular shapes during the annealing process. However, the IO-2 and IO-3 sample display a hydrangea-like structure with plentiful In2O3-based nanosheets (Fig. 3c and Fig. 3e), which could construct numerous pore structures and provide adequate space for diffusion channels. In addition, the well-defined and hydrangea-like morphology could also be observed by the closer SEM images. As shown in Fig. 4 b, a similar morphology can also be observed in the IO-2@CQDs sample.

To further clarify the detailed microscopic morphology and internal microstructure, the hydrangea-like IO-2@CQDs were characterized by the TEM and EDS mapping. The TEM image in Fig. 4c–d reveals that the hydrangea-like sample is composed of several In2O3 nanosheets, which are tightly and overlappingly grown on the micro-spherical space. It can be seen clearly that the average thickness of these nanosheets is about 20 nm. The highlighted dark walls and bright contrast located at the center confirm the loose nature of the as-prepared samples. As a matter of fact, the hydrangea-like structure could provide sufficient active site and work as a promising sensing structure for gas diffusion and gas-sensing reactions [11]. High-magnification TEM images of the pure CQDs at different magnifications are shown in Fig. 4e and Fig. S5a. As seen, CQDs display uniform and dispersed spherical particles and the diameter ranges from of 3–6 nm, which is consistent with the properties of CQDs [23]. The precise lattice fringe spacing of 0.291 nm and 0.253 nm are well agreed with the (222) and (400) lattice planes of cubic In2O3 (Fig. 4f and Fig. S5b). Subsequently, the carbon quantum dots cover or attached to the parallel lattice planes of In2O3 (luminous yellow marker), demonstrating the successful modification and formation of the 3D/0D heterostructured nanomaterials. Furthermore, the corresponding EDS images is displayed in Fig. 5 to investigate the element distributions. Notably, the co-existence and dispersion of C, O and In elements are exactly affirmed. It can be observed that the three elements display a hydrangea-like distribution, which is consistent with the TEM results. Additionally, combining the SEM and TEM images, one could find that In2O3-based samples possess relatively rough surfaces.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra was performed to investigate the functional groups. The FTIR spectra of carbon sphere template is presented in Fig. S6a and then described in detail in the Supplementary Material. Carbon spheres are first synthesized to serve as soft templates. The In ions are then attached to the carbon spheres by coordination or electrostatic interaction with hydrophilic groups (–OH, C–H and C-O groups) on the carbon spheres [24]. After calcination decomposition at 500 °C, nearly all the main peaks of template disappeared. The slight stretching vibration of CO, CC and CO bonds were due to the residues in In2O3 formed by the calcination procedure [25]. The peak at 3419 cm−1 in all samples appertains to the OH-hydrogen-bond stretching [26]. For pure In2O3 and IO-2@CQDs in Fig. S6b, the characteristic peaks at 602, 566, 539 and 442 cm−1 were assigned to In-O stretching vibrations of In2O3, respectively [27,28]. As displayed in the spectra of pure CQDs, the peaks at around 2954 and 2870 cm−1 are associated with C–H bond [29]. A peak appears at 1708 cm−1, which is the characteristic peak of CO stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1515 cm−1, the main characteristic vibrational band, is attributed to the stretching vibration of the CC group [23,25]. Furthermore, the peak at 1364 cm−1 and 1279 cm−1 was related to the stretching vibration of the COOH and C-O-C groups [23]. The peak at 1037 cm−1 is attributed to the C-O [22]. These characteristic peaks of CQDs all can also be found in the spectrum of IO-2@CQDs, confirm the successful incorporation of carbon quantum dots.

The recorded Raman spectra of these samples are also displayed in Fig. S7. For pure In2O3 and IO-2@CQDs, the typical bands at 130, 307, 364, 494 and 627 cm−1 are observed. They are attributed to In-O vibrations (130 cm−1), bending vibration of InO6 octahedra (307 cm−1), stretching vibration of In-O-In plane (364 cm−1) and stretching vibration modes of InO6 octahedrons (494 and 627 cm−1), respectively [7,30]. Two broad peaks located at around 1346 cm−1 and 1582 cm−1 can be also observed in CQDs (yellow plot) and IO-2@CQDs sample (red plot), which are indexed to the D (sp3 hybrid) and G (sp2 hybrid) bands of carbon quantum dots, implying the generation of aromatic carbon rings [31,32]. The characteristic bands of In2O3 and CQDs all can be observed in Raman spectrum of 3D/0D structure, which further confirmed the successful preparation of IO-2@CQDs.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the manufactured materials have been examined to distinguish the chemical states and composition. In the full-scan spectrum of Fig. S8a, C, In and O elements present. As shown in Fig. S8b, the peaks of carbon element can be fitted into three sub-peaks, which positioned at 284.7, 286.3 and 288.6 eV were fitted to the binding energies of CC, COH and CO bonds, respectively [33]. The CO, CC and CO bonds of IO-2 were due to the residues in In2O3 formed by the calcination procedure. The peak intensity of IO-2@CQDs is higher than that of pure In2O3 due to the functional groups of CQDs. Similarly, two major peaks in the In 3d spectra at 444.3 and 451.8 eV with a splitting width of 7.5 eV could be in good agreement with In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2 (Fig. S8c), demonstrating the characteristic of In3+ in In2O3 [25]. Compare pared with pure In2O3, the binding energy slightly shifts towards high binding energy. The electron transfer at the heterojunction interface (from In2O3 to CQDs) may be to blame for this shift, which decrease the electron cloud density of In2O3 [34,35]. Additionally, the original peak and post-fit XPS spectra of O 1 s were presented in Fig. S8d. Three individual peaks could correspond to OL (lattice oxygen), OV (oxygen vacancies), and OC (surface chemisorbed oxygen). It is well known that OC can impact the sensing properties. This is due to the fact that OC can provide more active sites for the adsorption and sensing reaction of gas molecules. The proportion of OC in IO-2@CQDs (56.49 %) is significantly higher than that of pure IO-2 (36.66 %), which can be attributed to the formation of the heterostructure.

Furthermore, the specific surface areas (SSA) and pore structure of the as-prepared samples are determined by the multi-point Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) methods. Based on the IUPAC classification, the isotherms could be classified as the IV isotherm with an apparent H3 hysteresis loop, indicating the existence of mesopores (Fig. S9). The SSA of IO-2 is about 23.77 m2/g. Similarly, the corresponding parameters for IO-2@CQDs nanocomposite is 38.56 m2/g. Meanwhile, some smaller pores (about 2.7–3.8 nm) exist in all samples, which could be ascribed to the small holes between well-connected nanoparticles or the gap between nanosheets. It may further increase the specific surface area, contributing to gas adsorption.

It is well-established that the operating temperature is an extremely significant parameter. Almost all the MOSs-based sensors exhibit excellent response capabilities at their respective optimum operating temperature (OOT). To obtain the optimal testing conditions, In2O3-based sensors were fabricated and measured (80–240 °C) upon exposure to 100-ppm formaldehyde gas (Fig. 6 a). It can be seen that nearly all the sample displays a “bell-shaped (growth-maximum-decrease)” correlation with operating temperature. According to the known theory, operating temperature affects the number of thermally excited electrons, carrier transportation, and adsorption–desorption equilibrium of gas molecules in the sensing layer [36]. Lower operating temperature does not provide sufficient activation energy for the reaction of oxygen species with formaldehyde molecules; however, inordinately elevated temperature led to adsorption saturation, at which time the desorption of the gas inhibits the adsorption process and thus reduces the reaction efficiency and utilization of the sensing layer. As a result, an OOT of 160 °C is selected for further sensing measurements. Meanwhile, when In ions are more abundant, excessively agglomerated nanosheets will decrease the response of the sensing layer. Notably, for IO-2@CQDs in Fig. 6a, the sensing response can be got as 36.79 at 160 °C, which is 6.35, 3.06 and 4.22 times larger than that of pristine IO-1, IO-2, and IO-3, respectively. It can be concluded that optimized morphology, regulated internal microstructure, and modification of CQDs are promising and valuable strategies for improving high-response sensing behaviors.

Fig. 6b reveal the initial resistance of Ra within a certain operating temperature. It turns out that the introduction of carbon quantum dots does increase the Ra (20.01 kΩ) at the same temperature, compared to the original material (16.77 kΩ). Surface defects and oxygen-containing functional groups in carbon materials affect the surface contact of the elements, as well as the generation of n-p heterojunction, which both inevitably increase resistance [19,37]. The initial resistance also gradually decreases with the increased operating temperatures; such phenomenon is identical to the behavior of semiconductor where the resistance decreases due to thermal excitation [38]. Meanwhile, the transient resistance variation under 160 °C are shown in Fig. 6c. When formaldehyde gas evaporates into the measurement chamber, the corresponding resistance exhibits a dynamic downward trend and then gradually reaches an equilibrium value. After exposed to air, the resistance recoveries shortly to the initial value, revealing a typical n-type semiconductor sensing-behavior.

Generally, the response-recovery characteristic is an essential factor for sensors. Six continuous circles of dynamic response curves were depicted in Fig. 6d–f at the optimum operating temperature. As seen, the response of all sensors maintains at a relatively stable level and then returns to its initial value with little change, indicating their good capability and reversible response. From Fig. 6f, one can observe that the IO-2@CQDs also exhibits excellent cycling stability. The response and recovery times (τ res /τ rec ) of four sensors are measured as 28/95 (IO-1), 20/78 (IO-2), 22/83 (IO-3), and 18/91 (IO-2@CQDs), respectively. In comparison, loose hydrangea-like In2O3 has the faster response and recovery speed than irregularly shaped nanoparticles. It can be found that the τ rec of all sensors was longer than τ res , which can be assigned to the chemisorption of formaldehyde, and reduction reaction products cannot be immediately desorbed. However, it is undeniable that the response and recovery times of IO-2@CQDs composites are slightly longer and need further improvement in future studies. The corresponding dynamic resistance curves of IO-2 and IO-2@CQDs are shown in Fig. S10. Their resistance amplitudes initially decreased, and then it can be almost fully restored to the original value after each measurement, which further confirms the n-type sensing characteristic.

Furthermore, a prominent response change tendency with formaldehyde concentration was also investigated. In both cases of Fig. 7 a–c, it can be obviously seen that the responses increase stepwise as the increasing concentration (20–400 ppm). However, the sensors reached the saturation state with a further increase in concentration (400 ppm). Besides, IO-2@CQDs exhibits an improved response at the whole concentration range, followed by the pure In2O3-based sensor (Fig. 7d–e). The dependence of response vs concentration can be directly reflected by the linear plots, as shown in Fig. 7f. The fitting coefficients of the four samples are 0.93, 0.99, 0.98, and 0.96, separately. Based on the equation of S1-S3 (Supplementary Material), the calculated lowest detection limit (LDL) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) for IO-2@CQDs are 86.23 and 287.45 ppb, which is in accordance with the TLV threshold of exposure set by the National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH, 1 ppm), World Health Organization (WHO, 1 ppm) and OSHA (0.75 ppm) [39,40]. Such a low LDL demonstrates the sufficient ability for formaldehyde recognition. As for other three In2O3-based sensors, the corresponding LDL and LOQ follows the order of IO-2 (242/809 ppb) < IO-3 (333/1112 ppb) < IO-1 (833/2777 ppb), revealing the enhanced gas-sensitive response values for hydrangea-like In2O3. On the basis of these results, the improvement of S exhibits the strong sensitization effect of heterojunction [41]. Long-term stability is crucial to the service life of MOSs sensors. A continuous sensing test was performed every two days within four weeks. As depicted in Fig. 8 a, it shows a slight fluctuation in response over the testing period, indicating reliable long-term stability.

In order to be suitable for accurate recognition and early warning of formaldehyde gas, selectivity is evaluated in Fig. 8b–d and Fig. S10. All In2O3-based gas sensors were exposed to five available interfering gases (100 ppm) and subsequently tested at optimal operating temperature. Clearly, for 100 ppm C3H8O, gas responses were recorded as 5.92 and 8.85 to IO-2 and IO-2@CQDs. Conversely, poor responses to the other four gases were also observed, revealing the preferable selectivity to formaldehyde gas. To facilitate comparison, the selectivity coefficient (Q) of SF /SG (with a subscript G representing interfering gases) is introduced to evaluate the selectivity further. Then the corresponding Q for IO-2@CQDs are 7.53, 5.95, 13.67, 8.5 and 4.17, respectively. As for IO-2, the selectivity coefficient shows the same order as 5.39, 3.49, 7.9, 6.26, and 2.25, both endorsing the remarkable selectivity to formaldehyde. One of the reasons for superior selectivity may be that the bond dissociation energy of formaldehyde is smaller than that of isopropanol (381 kJ·mol−1), ethanol (436.5 kJ·mol−1), acetone (401.2 kJ·mol−1), benzene (473 kJ·mol−1) and ammonia (450 kJ·mol−1). The smaller the bond dissociation energy, the easier it is to break the bond and participate in the reaction [42]. In the case of mesoporous nanomaterials, the Knudsen diffusion dominates in three kinds of diffusion (molecule diffusion, surface diffusion, and Knudsen diffusion) without external pressure, as shown in Eq. (3):
(3)
D k
=
4 r
3
2 R T
π M
in which, the Dk , r, R, T, and M represent diffusion coefficient, pore diameter, gas constant, absolute temperature (in Kelvin) and molecular weight, respectively [37]. The smaller the molecule weight, the greater the Knudsen diffusion coefficient. As shown in Table S1, the molecule weight and kinetic diameter of HCHO (2.43 Å) is smaller. It readily passes through small pore apertures, facilitating the rapid transportation and selective adsorption at the hetero-interface [42,43].

For a better comparison to demonstrate the advantages of our as-prepared materials, the HCHO-sensing behaviors between IO-2@CQDs nanocomposites and various kinds of sensing materials have been summarized in Table 1
[45–50]. Noticeably, the current IO-2@CQDs sample outperforms the formaldehyde sensor, including moderate optimum operating temperature, sensing capacity in low concentration, and a higher response value. Its HCHO-sensing performance was nothing short of outstanding except for the recovery time. Based on the above analysis, the CQDs-modified hydrangea-like In2O3 could serve as a novel sensing-material for detecting formaldehyde indoors and outdoors.

To elucidate the adsorption behavior of gas molecules on the surface of In2O3 and 3D/0D heterojunction, 11 models have been established and then calculated to investigate the adsorption energy and charge transfer properties. The specific (222) plane of cubic phase In2O3 and CQDs was chosen to build the optimal surface structure (Fig. S12), determined by the XRD characteristics, TEM lattice spacing, and previous literature [51,52]. Moreover, two different adsorption position (In- and O-position) was considered and estimated in all adsorption systems to investigate the optimal geometry structure.

As we all know, the larger the Eads is, the easier it is to adsorb onto the slab surface. It can be seen from Table S2 (Supplementary Material) that the Eads of oxygen molecules (0.1805 and 0.5302 eV) are positive for pure In2O3, demonstrating the heat-adsorbing properties. It also strongly suggests that the In2O3 sensor must be operated at a specific temperature to effectively adsorb oxygen for resistance modulation. In contrast, the adsorption energy of formaldehyde molecules on In-position and O-position structures are −0.7053 and −0.3095 eV (Fig. 9 (g1)–(h1) and Fig. S13 (g)–(h)), respectively. All the Eads of HCHO on the (222) surface is negative, meaning that the gas is easy to chemisorb spontaneously, and the adsorption is an exothermic process.

For gaining a deep understanding to uncover the role of CQDs modification, the corresponding IO-2@CQDs heterojunction has also been established. Notably, the Eads of IO-2@CQDs (-0.7538 and −0.8801 eV) are much higher than that of pure (222) In2O3, which directly represents a better adsorption performance for HCHO. Simultaneously, the adsorption capability of 3D/0D heterojunction for oxygen was also strengthened (-0.2231 and −0.8493 eV). Furthermore, Mulliken population analysis (MPA) and charge difference density (CDD) were also calculated to describe the charge transfer properties and then represented in Fig. 9, Fig. S13 and Table S2. Obviously, 0.015–0.208 e- are gained by the oxygen, that is, oxygen serves as the electron acceptor on (222) In2O3. It is worth noting that the oxygen gains 0.237 (In-position) e- and 0.348 e- (O-position) from the heterojunction interface, implying a higher activity for O2 adsorption than pristine In2O3. Meanwhile, the corresponding MPA of HCHO on two adsorption sites of (222) In2O3 are 0.105 and 0.075 e-. Similarly, abundant electrons (0.094 and 0.09 e-) transfer from adsorbed formaldehyde molecules to the 3D/0D heterojunction, indicating the electron donor nature of reducing gas. Since the charge carrier of n-type MOSs is mainly electron, the resistance will decrease in ambient formaldehyde, which matches well with the previous experimental results (Fig. 6c and Fig. S10). IO-2@CQDs exhibits stronger electron migration capability for formaldehyde adsorption, which plays a leading part in the enhanced sensing performance. Fig. 9 (a2)–(h2) and Fig. S14 intuitively display the CDD distribution images of the adsorption models, where red and blue region indicate the effective positive and negative charge, respectively. The corresponding electron cloud distribution of CDD in the HCHO-adsorption model is nonpolar, exhibiting covalent bonding characteristics. It also can be seen that the donated electrons from HCHO are mainly concentrated (red electron cloud) on the substrate surface. In contrast, the red region around oxygen molecules suggests that they can preferentially obtain electrons, which is consistent with MPA calculation.

To further clarify the selectivity, the adsorption energy for other five interfering gases has also been calculated. As displayed in Fig. S15 and Table S3, the HCHO molecules shows the highest Eads compared to other VOCs molecules, indicating the stronger affinity of hetero-interface for formaldehyde. In view of the above calculation, it is expected that IO-2@CQDs is more sensitive than cubic In2O3 when used for formaldehyde detection.

Based on previous literature, the generally accepted gas sensing mechanism of MOSs can be clarified by the surface depletion theory, which is attributed to the resistance modulation induced by the reduction reaction between adsorbed oxygen species and gas molecules. To further reveal the sensing mechanism, we performed the O2-TPD characterization to study the surface-adsorbed oxygen species. Generally, the desorption capability of various oxygen species on metal oxide surface is as follows: O2−(ads) < O−(ads) < O2 −(ads) < O2(ads) [40]. Therefore, the desorption peak below 200 °C is attributed to the desorption of surface oxygen (O2(ads)), as shown in Fig. 10 a. And two other desorption peaks located at the range of 300–500 °C, are separately attributed to the desorption of chemically adsorbed oxygen species (O2 −(ads) and O−(ads)) [53,54]. The fourth desorption peaks at 551–603 °C correspond to the lattice oxygen species (O2−(ads)) [55]. Obviously, the integral desorption peak area of IO-2@CQDs is significantly larger than that of IO-2, indicating more oxygen species is generated, which is consistent with the obtained XPS results. Correspondingly, the O2-TPD profile proves that IO-2 and IO-2@CQDs interface quickly adsorb oxygen molecules in air and participates in the gas-sensitive reaction to generate massive O2 −(ads) and O−(ads) with the increment of working temperature. The dominant oxygen species is O−(ads) at the OOT of 160 °C [54].

In2O3, as a representative n-type MOSs, contains a lot of electrons as the primary current carrier. Based on the above O2-TPD, when as-prepared In2O3-based sensors are exposed to ambient air (Fig. 11 a), the atmospheric O2 molecules are adsorbed on the surface, thus capturing free electrons from the conducting band (ECB) and ionize to negatively charged surface-adsorbed oxygen species, such as O2 –, O- or O2–. Typically, oxygen is adsorbed in the form of O- at 160 °C, as shown in Eqs. (4)–(7):

Through this process, an electron depletion layer (EDL) and a potential barrier (ΔΦ1) are generated. The corresponding energy bend is upward bending, narrowing the electron-transporting channel and increasing nanomaterial resistance. When exposed to formaldehyde, formaldehyde molecules undergo the chemical oxidation reaction with adsorbed oxygen species and are converted to carbon dioxide and water molecules. Then, the captured electron will be released back to the sensing layer, weakening the thickness of EDL and ΔΦ1, which are represented in the following equations:

The above oxidation reaction on the interface resulting in the widening the electron-transporting channel, which eventually leads to the decrease in sensor resistance.

Compared to bare In2O3, the improvement of HCHO-sensing properties of hydrangea-like IO-2@CQDs can be accounted for in the following aspects: enhanced electronic characteristics, n-p heterojunction, unique morphology, and active adsorption sites. First of all, Mott-Schottky (M−S) experiments, UV–vis spectroscopy and EIS measurements were carried out. Fig. 10b and Fig. S16 display the Mott-Schottky diagrams for pure In2O3, CQDs and IO-2@CQDs. The positive slope of the M−S plot confirms the n-type conductivity of In2O3. Similarly, the negative slope of CQDs suggests the p-type semiconductor characteristic [56]. However, the IO-2@CQDs composite show typical n-type semiconductor properties, probably due to the low doping content of the CQDs, which is similar to the literature where the M−S curves of n-p heterojunctions show only n-type or p-type semiconductor behaviors [55,57,58]. The n-type properties of IO-2@CQDs is also consistent with the results of sensing-performance measurement. Based on the follow Mott-Schottky formula: Nd = (-2/qεε0)((1/C2)/dv)−1 (where q, ε, ε0, 1/C2 is the fundamental charge constant, relative permittivity of In2O3, vacuum permittivity and slope of M−S plot) [59]. The calculated carrier concentration of pure IO-2 and IO-2@CQDs are 7.66 × 1020 cm−3 and 8.71 × 1020 cm−3, respectively. It confirms the rate of charge transfer is enhanced. The electron transfers from In2O3 to CQDs, reducing the electron density of In2O3 [60]. Combined with the surface defects and oxygen-containing functional groups in CQDs, the resistance of IO-2@CQDs increase, which is consistent with the obtained resistance curve. Meanwhile, IO-2 exhibits strong absorption of UV light with a band gap of 2.76 eV (Fig. 10c–d), which is similarly with the reported work [61]. Tauc plots (Fig. 10e, curve) reveal that CQDs modification enables to narrow the band gap to 2.53 (IO-2@CQDs), which can promote electron transfer and contribute to the sensing performance. The EIS spectrums can be seen in Fig. 10f. It is obvious that the IO-2@CQDs sensor has the smallest circular arc radius at the high frequency region, which indicates the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 3D/0D structure is the fastest, further confirming the higher electron transportation efficiency of heterointerface. It can be concluded that the IO-2@CQDs sensor possesses the better electronic properties. The electro-analysis also strongly supports the above-mentioned DFT calculations (more electron transfer number). The admirable electronic properties are beneficial for the enhanced HCHO-sensing performance that in turn is naturally related to the composition and 3D/0D structure of IO-2@CQDs heterojunction.

Furthermore, the work function of In2O3 (4.2 eV) is smaller than that of carbon quantum dots (4.5 eV) [62,63]. Once two materials come into contact, electron tend to flow from In2O3 region to CQDs region until the Fermi energy levels (EF) are in equilibrium state, allowing formation of the potential barrier (ΔΦ2), which lead to a higher electron transfer efficiency and contributes further to the energy bend bending (Fig. 11b). Moreover, carbon materials could serve as the electron shuttled mediator to accelerate the electron transfer between In2O3 sensing layer and gas molecules [64]. Ultimately, an extra electron depletion layer would be formed on the hetero-interface, increasing the height of barrier (Fig. 11a, ΔΦ2 > ΔΦ1). As for the IO-2@CQDs sensor, the resistance is much higher than that of pristine In2O3, which is a corollary of the formation of n-p heterojunction. IO-2@CQDs with abundant surface chemisorbed oxygen can play a crucial role in the adsorption and sensing reaction of formaldehyde. When IO-2@CQDs sensors is exposed to reductive HCHO gas, electrons generated by the oxidation reactions of formaldehyde will be denoted back to the ECB of the sensing layer, resulting in a drastic reduction of resistance. According to the response definition (Ra/Rg), a higher Ra will be more favorable to the response enhancement. Hence, the response of the as-prepared 3D/0D sensor is improved due to the enhanced baseline resistance [65].

As a matter of fact, the heterojunction will also generate a great deal of grain boundary barrier, including not only the In2O3-CQDs barrier but also the In2O3-In2O3 and CQDs-CQDs barrier (Fig. 11c) [66]. A sharp significant shrink of the electron depletion layer and potential barrier height for the IO-2@CQDs hybrid system will occur in ambient formaldehyde. Based on the equation (
Δ R ∝
R 0
e x p
q Δ Φ / k T
, where ΔR refers to the variation of resistance, R0 is the initial resistance, q represents the electronic charge; ΔΦ is the potential barrier height, k represents the Boltzmann constant (1.38 × 10-23 J/K), and T is the absolute temperature (in Kelvin).). It is expected that a slight fluctuation in the ΔΦ could drastically lead to a large variation of sensor resistance, thus attaining superior sensing capacity [67,68]. Although the difference in potential barrier height of In2O3-In2O3 (ΔΦ1) and CQDs-CQDs (ΔΦ3) is smaller than that of In2O3-CQDs (ΔΦ2), it still makes an additional contribution to sensing properties [69].

On the other hand, the unique three-dimensional hydrangea-like morphology assembled from In2O3 nanosheets can expose abundant adsorption sites and provide more gas diffusion channels, making adsorption easier. Clearly, CQDs modification endows hydrangea-like In2O3 with the largest SSA. Diffusion channels constructed by voids between In2O3 nanosheets and a large number of mesopores pave the way for diffusion and facile penetration of gas molecules to the inner space (Fig. 11d), leading to rapid intermolecular reactions, thereby enhancing HCHO sensing performance.

Finally, In2O3 has excellent conductivity; it can offer a great deal of In- and O-site for oxygen and formaldehyde adsorption. Meanwhile, the small size of carbon quantum dots does dot not only effectively increase the SSA but also boost the HCHO-sensing performance through effectively enhanced adsorption energy and electron transmission. This facilitates the strong gas adsorption capacity of oxygen (-0.2231 eV/-0.348 e- and −0.8493 eV/-0.237 e-) and formaldehyde (-0.7538 eV/0.094 e- and −0.8801 eV/0.09 e-) molecule, and making more intense sensing reactions happen through enhanced electron migration number.

Based on the above analysis, it can be speculated that the improved HCHO-sensing performance can be contribute to a synergistic effect of electronic characteristics, n-p heterojunction, unique morphology and active surface sites.

In summary, the highly sensitive formaldehyde sensors using hydrangea-like In2O3 and CQDs-functionalized In2O3 are reported. Meanwhile, the addition molar ratio has been optimized to understand the effect of morphology evolution further. The constructed interior cavity and relatively rough surface of 3D/0D IO-2@CQDs with mesoporous properties show a large surface area, providing numerous diffusion channels and active surface sites to guarantee the effective gas diffusion, penetration and reaction. Benefitting from the hydrangea-like morphology and n-p heterojunction, the IO-2@CQDs sensor exhibits acceptable stability, excellent selectivity, and a low detection limit of 86.23 ppb, demonstrating good sensing performance. Subsequently, the CQDs sensitization mechanism is explained by considering the DFT calculation and surface depletion theory. Compared with the pure In2O3, CQDs modification strengthens the adsorption ability and electron migration capability, which proves that the surface functionalization of CQDs modification is more beneficial to the intriguing gas-sensing properties. This work sheds light on a novel design strategy of 3D/0D hybrid structures for high-performance HCHO gas sensors.
